01-cd52-analysis

Sam Chen

2026-06-30

  • Set up
  • I. Load and clean data
  • II. Confirm CD52 expression patterns in samples
  • III. Annotate main T cell subtypes
  • IV. Check non-CD52 markers in CD52+ unedited vs CD52- edited T cell subtypes
  • V. Conclusion
  • Session Info

Set up

I. Load and clean data

load_conway_cd52_data <- function(sample){
  
  sample_name <- gsub("_", "-", sample)
  
  cd52pos <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, sample, paste0(sample_name, "-CD52pos.csv"))) %>%
    mutate(CD52_status = "CD52+")
  cd52neg <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, sample, paste0(sample_name, "-CD52neg.csv"))) %>%
    mutate(CD52_status = "CD52-")
  
  df <- bind_rows(cd52pos, cd52neg)
  df_long <- df %>%
    select(file, CD52_status, matches("^Cluster.*\\.median$")) %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols = matches("^Cluster.*\\.median$"),
      names_to = "feature",
      values_to = "value"
    ) %>%
    tidyr::extract(
      feature,
      into = c("cluster", "channel", "marker"),
      regex = "^(Cluster\\d+)\\.(.+)___(.+)\\.median$"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::rename(condition = file) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      condition = case_when(
        grepl("non[- ]?edited|unedited", condition, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "unedited",
        grepl("^edited| edited", condition, ignore.case = TRUE) ~ "edited",
        TRUE ~ condition
      ),
      sample = sample) %>% 
    dplyr::filter(marker != "Viability")
  
  df_long
}

samples <- c("CD52KO_BC0610", "CD52KO_BC8599", "CD52KO_BC9507")

df_list <- lapply(samples, load_conway_cd52_data)
names(df_list) <- samples

II. Confirm CD52 expression patterns in samples

This plot confirms that CD52 expression is high in the CD52+ samples and low in the CD52- samples, as expected. CD52 expressions are also generally lower in edited samples compared to unedited.

cd52_df <- bind_rows(df_list) %>%
  dplyr::filter(marker == "CD52")

ggplot(cd52_df, aes(x = sample, y = value, fill = condition)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~CD52_status) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "CD52 expression across clusters and conditions",
    x = "Sample",
    y = "Median CD52 expression"
  ) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
## Warning: Removed 23 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_bar()`).

III. Annotate main T cell subtypes

I roughly annotated clusters into main T cell subtypes (CD4, CD8, gamma-delta) based on the mean expression of key markers in each cluster. This is a rough annotation and may not be perfect, but it should help us check if the expected T cell subtypes are present in the CD52+ vs CD52- samples.

annotate_tcell_subtypes <- function(df) {
  cluster_anno <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(marker %in% c("CD3", "CD4", "CD8", "TCRab", "TCRgd")) %>%
    group_by(sample, condition, CD52_status, cluster, marker) %>%
    summarise(value = mean(value), .groups = "drop") %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = marker, values_from = value) %>%
    mutate(
      cell_type = case_when(
        is.na(CD3) | CD3 <= 0 ~ "non-T/unclear",
        TCRgd > TCRab ~ "gamma-delta T",
        TCRab >= TCRgd & CD4 > CD8 ~ "CD4 T",
        TCRab >= TCRgd & CD8 > CD4 ~ "CD8 T",
        TRUE ~ "unknown"
      )
    )
  
  cluster_anno
}

df_long <- bind_rows(df_list)
cluster_annotations <- annotate_tcell_subtypes(df_long)

df_annot <- df_long %>%
  left_join(
    cluster_annotations %>%
      select(sample, condition, CD52_status, cluster, cell_type),
    by = c("sample", "condition", "CD52_status", "cluster")
  )

Z-score heatmap of key T cell markers across clusters, faceted by sample, condition, and CD52 status. This should help us visually confirm the expected expression patterns of CD3, CD4, CD8, TCRab, and TCRgd in the annotated clusters.

heatmap_df <- cluster_annotations %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(CD3, CD4, CD8, TCRab, TCRgd),
    names_to = "marker",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  group_by(sample, condition, CD52_status, marker) %>%
  mutate(zscore = as.numeric(scale(value))) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(heatmap_df, aes(x = marker, y = cluster, fill = zscore)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_grid(sample ~ condition + CD52_status, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", midpoint = 0) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

This plot shows the mean expression of CD4, CD8, and TCRgd across clusters, faceted by annotated cell type. This confirms that the clusters annotated as CD4 T cells have higher CD4 expression, and those annotated as CD8 T cells have higher CD8 expression.

ggplot(cluster_annotations %>%
         pivot_longer(c(CD4, CD8, TCRgd),
                      names_to="marker",
                      values_to="value"),
       aes(cluster, value, fill=marker)) +
  geom_col(position="dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~cell_type) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )
## Warning: Removed 69 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_col()`).

IV. Check non-CD52 markers in CD52+ unedited vs CD52- edited T cell subtypes

Facet grid of non-CD52 marker asingh normalized expression across samples, cell types, and conditions. This enabled visual inspections of any non-CD52 markers that show consistent differences between CD52+ unedited vs CD52- edited samples within CD4 and CD8 T cell subtypes. There are very few outliers at first glance.

compare_df <- df_annot %>%
  dplyr::filter(
    (CD52_status == "CD52+" & condition == "unedited") |
    (CD52_status == "CD52-" & condition == "edited")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    group = case_when(
      CD52_status == "CD52+" & condition == "unedited" ~ "CD52+ control",
      CD52_status == "CD52-" & condition == "edited" ~ "CD52- KO"),
    value_asinh = asinh(value / 1000)
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::filter(cell_type %in% c("CD4 T", "CD8 T"))

ggplot(compare_df %>% dplyr::filter(marker != "CD52"),
       aes(marker, value_asinh, color = group)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_grid(sample ~ cell_type)+
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Here I calculated the mean asinh-transformed expression of each protein marker in CD52+ unedited vs CD52- edited samples, and plotted them against each other. The red points indicate CD52 itself, which shows the expected pattern of high expression in CD52+ unedited and low expression in CD52- edited samples. Most other markers cluster around the diagonal, indicating similar expression levels between the two groups.

summary_df <- compare_df %>%
  group_by(sample, marker, group) %>%
  summarise(mean_asinh = mean(value_asinh), .groups="drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = group, values_from = mean_asinh) %>%
  mutate(CD52 = marker == "CD52")

ggplot(summary_df,
       aes(`CD52+ control`, `CD52- KO`, color=CD52)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey60", "TRUE" = "red")) +
  facet_wrap(~sample) +
  theme_bw() + labs(x = "CD52+ unedited", y = "CD52- edited (KO)")

I performed Wilcoxon rank-sum tests to compare the asinh-transformed expression of each marker between CD52+ unedited and CD52- edited clusters within each T cell subtypes and sample. The results are adjusted for multiple testing using the FDR method. The top 10 markers with the smallest adjusted p-values are shown below, and the full results are saved to a CSV file. CD52 itself shows a highly significant difference, as expected, while most other markers do not show significant differences. A few non-CD52 markers exhibiting nomimal significance may warrant further investigation, but overall there do not appear to be strong consistent differences in non-CD52 markers between the CD52+ unedited and CD52- edited samples within the main T cell subtypes.

wilcox_results <- compare_df %>%
  group_by(sample, cell_type, marker) %>%
  summarise(
    p_value = wilcox.test(value_asinh ~ group, exact=FALSE)$p.value,
    control_mean = mean(value_asinh[group == "CD52+ control"]),
    KO_mean = mean(value_asinh[group == "CD52- KO"]),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(p_value, method = "fdr"))

wilcox_results %>%
  arrange(p_adj) %>% top_n(10, wt = -p_adj)
## # A tibble: 10 × 7
##    sample        cell_type marker    p_value control_mean KO_mean   p_adj
##    <chr>         <chr>     <chr>       <dbl>        <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
##  1 CD52KO_BC0610 CD8 T     CD52   0.00000747        5.27    1.59  0.00170
##  2 CD52KO_BC9507 CD8 T     CD52   0.0000390         5.66    1.43  0.00444
##  3 CD52KO_BC0610 CD4 T     CD52   0.0000873         5.53    1.48  0.00498
##  4 CD52KO_BC8599 CD4 T     CD52   0.0000873         5.80    1.51  0.00498
##  5 CD52KO_BC9507 CD4 T     CD52   0.000124          5.82    1.54  0.00566
##  6 CD52KO_BC8599 CD8 T     CD52   0.000248          5.52    1.70  0.00941
##  7 CD52KO_BC8599 CD4 T     CD107a 0.000419         -0.123   1.56  0.0137 
##  8 CD52KO_BC9507 CD4 T     CXCR2  0.000491         -1.45   -0.206 0.0140 
##  9 CD52KO_BC9507 CD8 T     CXCR2  0.000779         -1.19    0.107 0.0197 
## 10 CD52KO_BC9507 CD8 T     TIM3   0.000959         -0.405   0.295 0.0219
write.csv(wilcox_results %>% arrange(p_adj), file.path(results_dir, "wilcox_results.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

V. Conclusion

  • CD52 expression is high in CD52+ samples and low in CD52- samples, confirming the expected patterns.
  • The main T cell subtypes (CD4, CD8, gamma-delta) are present in both CD52+ and CD52- samples, with expected marker expression patterns.
  • There are very few non-CD52 markers that show consistent differences between CD52+ unedited and CD52- edited samples within the CD4+ and CD8+ T cell subtypes, suggesting that CD52 KO may not have strong effects on the expression of other surface markers in these samples.
